content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init**: at iter start, `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` is created so the plan + review agents have a stable destination for their per-iter narrative (`plan.md`, `review.md`, optional `objectives.md`). Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded across iters because per-iter content lives in the sidecars.
150	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
151	
152	`sync_leanok` writes an inner-git commit (`archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so its output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Review Agent — Post-Session Proof Journal + Analysis
2	
3	You are the review agent. Your job is to: (1) analyze the most recent prover session with fine-grained detail, (2) produce a structured proof journal, (3) update project status, (4) maintain the blueprint markers that require *semantic* judgement (`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations), and (5) write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	`\leanok` placement is handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs immediately before you. **Do not add or remove `\leanok` yourself.** If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the underlying Lean source still has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
6	
7	**Do NOT modify any .lean files. Do NOT write proofs.** You may run `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` to verify compilation and sorry counts as part of your analysis. Otherwise you only read logs, analyze, and write journal/status/blueprint-marker files.
8	
9	## Iteration number — canonical
10	
11	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. As of 2026 these are the **same number** — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN` (i.e. M == NNN). Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) when referencing the iteration in prose (`summary.md` metadata, recommendations titles like "Recommendations for the next plan-agent iteration (iter-{NNN+1:03d})") and the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory. If older `summary.md` / `recommendations.md` files use the legacy independent session counter, treat them as drift from earlier agents — your new file uses the iteration number.
12	
13	## Step 1: Identify Context
14	
15	1. Check `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/` — count existing session folders to determine the current session number.
16	2. Run `find ${PROJECT_PATH} -name '*.lean' -not -path '*/.lake/*' -not -path '*/lake-packages/*' | xargs grep -c 'sorry' 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$' | awk -F: '{s+=$2} END {print s}'` for the current sorry count.
17	3. Run `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` to see what changed.
18	
19	## Step 2: Read Pre-processed Attempt Data (MANDATORY)
20	
21	**READ `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` COMPLETELY.** If this file does not exist or is empty, report it and proceed with what you can gather from task_results.
22	
23	The file contains:
24	
25	- Line 1: Summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors, files edited)
26	- Remaining lines: One event per tool call — edits, goal states, errors, lemma searches, builds
27	
28	**For each `code_change` event**: Record the actual code that was tried (old_text → new_text).
29	**For each `goal_state` event**: Record the Lean goal at that point.
30	**For each `diagnostics` event**: Record the Lean errors.
31	**For each `build` event**: Record whether it succeeded or failed.
32	
33	This is your PRIMARY data source. Task result files are supplementary.
34	
35	## Step 3: Read Recent History
36	
37	If previous session folders exist in `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/`, read `summary.md` from the **last 2 sessions**. Also read `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
38	
39	## Step 4: Write Proof Journal
40	
41	Create the session folder and write two files:
42	
43	```bash
44	mkdir -p .archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>
45	```
46	
47	### File A: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/summary.md`
48	
49	Must include:
50	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted)
51	- For EACH target attempted:
52	  - **Every significant attempt** with: tactic/code tried, Lean error received, goal state at that point
53	  - What was learned from each failed attempt
54	  - For solved targets: the final proof structure with key lemmas
55	- Key findings / proof patterns discovered
56	- Recommendations for next session
57	
58	### File B: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/milestones.jsonl`
59	
60	Each line MUST follow this JSON format — one entry per target theorem:
61	
62	```json
63	{
64	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
65	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
66	  "target": {
67	    "file": "path/to/File.lean",
68	    "theorem": "theorem_name"
69	  },
70	  "session": {
71	    "id": "session_N",
72	    "model": "model-name"
73	  },
74	  "findings": {
75	    "blocker": "description if blocked",
76	    "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1", "lemma2"]
77	  },
78	  "attempts": [
79	    {
80	      "attempt": 1,
81	      "strategy": "what was tried",
82	      "code_tried": "actual Lean code or tactic",
83	      "line_number": "line number in the initial file where this code was attempted",
84	      "lean_error": "actual error message if failed",
85	      "goal_before": "the goal state before this attempt",
86	      "goal_after": "the goal state after this attempt",
87	      "result": "success|failed|partial",
88	      "insight": "what was learned from this attempt"
89	    }
90	  ],
91	  "next_steps": "..."
92	}
93	```
94	
95	**CRITICAL**: The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from the pre-processed data:
96	- If `attempts_raw.jsonl` shows 5 edits to a file, there should be multiple attempts recorded
97	- Each attempt must include `code_tried` (from edit events) and `lean_error` (from diagnostic events)
98	- Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one
99	
100	### File C: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/recommendations.md`
101	
102	Write concrete recommendations for the next plan agent iteration:
103	- Which targets are closest to completion and should be prioritized
104	- Which approaches showed promise but need more work
105	- Which targets are blocked and why (the plan agent should NOT assign these)
106	- Any reusable proof patterns discovered
107	
108	If your analysis shows the prover has hit the exact same blocker for several consecutive iterations on the same target, you should explicitly instruct the Plan Agent to avoid retrying the same approach without putting more effort into understanding the underlying issue.
109	
110	## Step 5: Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
111	
112	The per-session narrative (Overall Progress: total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` — see the next subsection. PROJECT_STATUS.md carries only the cumulative Knowledge Base:
113	
114	```markdown
115	# Project Status
116	
117	## Knowledge Base
118	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
119	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
120	
121	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
122	- <target>: <reason>
123	
124	## Last Updated
125	<ISO timestamp>
126	```
127	
128	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries an "Overall Progress" section from a prior project that grew it, leave the old content where it is (do not delete history) but stop appending to it. New session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
129	
130	### Per-iteration sidecars (split write)
131	
132	Your invocation prompt contains a `## Per-iteration sidecars` block that names `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` as the destination for THIS session's narrative. Split your writes accordingly:
133	
134	- **The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**, NOT to PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Overall Progress" section. PROJECT_STATUS.md no longer carries a session log.
135	- **You DO keep updating PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Knowledge Base" section.** Cumulative non-obvious facts (errors not to reproduce, reusable proof patterns, Mathlib idioms that worked) still belong in the Knowledge Base. The Knowledge Base is the only growing-but-curated part of PROJECT_STATUS.md.
136	- **Format for `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**: same fields as the Overall Progress block above (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, plus any narrative analysis). Born-bounded — this file contains THIS session only, never a multi-session log.
137	- Older `iter/iter-MMM/review.md` files are on disk for you to read on demand, but the recent window is already injected in your prompt.
138	
139	## Step 6: Blueprint Markers
140	
141	`\leanok` placement is now handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs between the prover and you. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration in the Lean source, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. **Do not touch `\leanok` markers yourself.** If you see one missing where you expect it, the underlying file probably doesn't compile or still has a sorry — do not paper over that with a manual edit.
142	
143	Your remaining marker responsibilities are the ones that require semantic judgement:
144	
145	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement-block only) — declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias / direct reference). The deterministic script never adds or removes this; you decide based on the prover's task result.
146	- **`\lean{...}` macro maintenance** — if a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, their task result will mention it. Update the `\lean{...}` line in the chapter to the correct Lean name. If a declaration was moved to a different file by a refactor, update the chapter's location references as needed.
147	- **`% NOTE: <reason>` annotations** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, add a `% NOTE: ...` comment explaining the obstacle so the plan agent sees it.
148	- **Stripping `\notready`** — if a `\notready` marker still sits on a block that the prover has now landed, remove it. The deterministic script does not manage `\notready`.
149	
150	### `\mathlibok` rules (your domain)
151	
152	Add `\mathlibok` inside the statement block when:
153	- The Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or a simple `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`), AND
154	- The Archon-side declaration itself contains no `sorry` and introduces no new proof obligation.
155	
156	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave the proof block alone if there's no Lean proof body to verify.
157	
158	### Record what you changed
159	
160	In `session_<N>/summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic script's `\leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]` and don't need to appear here):
161	
162	```markdown
163	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
164	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
165	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
166	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
167	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
168	```
169	
170	### File D: `.archon/TO_USER.md`
171	
172	While the agents should be autonomous, you might want to inform the user of any critical issues that require their attention. The content of `TO_USER.md` will be surfaced in the UI as an alert banner, however the user might not see it immediately or never see it at all (its content will be refreshed every iteration before the review agent is called). It might include for instance issues with the environment, critical missing dependencies, required user actions, etc. 
173	
174	**Always check the planner's iter sidecar for escalations.** Read `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` (this iter's plan-agent narrative). If the planner intentionally skipped provers — signaled by the `(no prover dispatch this iter ...)` marker in `PROGRESS.md`'s `## Current Objectives` section, by a "user escalation" / "hard gate fired" notice in the sidecar, or by the planner's `planValidate.status: ok_intentional_skip` meta — you MUST write a concise alert banner to `TO_USER.md` naming:
175	- What the impasse is (one sentence).
176	- What options the planner has surfaced (one bullet per option, if applicable).
177	- Where to find full context (the sidecar path).
178	
179	The planner does NOT write to `USER_HINTS.md` for escalations — `USER_HINTS.md` is user-to-planner only. The escalation banner is your responsibility; you are the user-facing channel.
180	
181	**Rules:**
182	- Be extremely concise (1-2 sentences per item, listed in markdown format).
183	- If nothing relevant for the user is detected, leave the file completely empty.
184	
185	## Step 7: Dispatch review subagents
186	
187	You may dispatch read-only review subagents BEFORE writing your consolidated summary. They are audits whose findings you incorporate into `summary.md` / `recommendations.md`. Each runs as its own fresh-context Claude process; spawning two or more in one assistant message runs them in parallel, subject to the global `max_parallel` cap.
188	
189	### Where the catalog comes from
190	
191	Your invocation prompt contains an auto-generated **Available subagents** section listing every enabled descriptor. Reviewers are typically marked `[read-only]`. A descriptor tagged `[MANDATORY]` for this phase MUST be dispatched before the phase completes. Do not `ls .archon/subagents/` — the catalog you were handed is authoritative.
192	
193	When you decide to invoke a specific reviewer, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
194	
195	### How to dispatch
196	
197	Pattern (Bash tool, parallel-able, run **in foreground** so the report lands before you write your summary):
198	
199	```
200	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
201	  --name <subagent-name> \
202	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
203	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
204	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
205	```
206	
207	Each directive must be **fully self-contained** — the reviewer reads only what the directive points it at. The directive shape for each reviewer is documented in its descriptor under `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
208	
209	### When NOT to dispatch
210	
211	- If this session was a pure proof-filling round with no new definitions or refactors, skip reviewers. They add latency and cost for no value.
212	- If a previous session in the last 3 iters already ran the same audit on the same scope, don't repeat unless that scope changed.
213	- The plan agent may also have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for matching reports before doubling up.
214	
215	### Incorporating findings
216	
217	After the reviewers return, read every report. Land each finding in your `summary.md` and `recommendations.md`:
218	
219	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** findings → bullet at the top of `recommendations.md` with the suggested action.
220	- **MEDIUM** findings → bullet in `recommendations.md`'s body.
221	- **LOW** findings → one-liner in `summary.md`'s notes section.
222	
223	Do NOT repeat the full report content in your summary — link to the report file. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.

57	### Detailed plan for iter-124 (Step 2 + Step 3)
58	
59	The residual `sorry` requires constructing the backward map and
60	verifying inverse identities. Recommended decomposition:
61	
62	1. **Step 2a — basic-open-cover helper**: prove that for every
63	   `W : Opens S` with `f.base ⁻¹ᵁ W ⊇ V` (i.e., `fV ⊆ W`), there
64	   exists `g ∈ appLE_unitSubmonoid f hU hV e` with `S.basicOpen g ⊆
65	   W ∩ U`. The argument uses:
66	   - Quasi-compactness of `f V` (Mathlib:
67	     `IsCompact.elim_finite_subcover` via `IsAffineOpen.isCompact`
68	     applied to `hV` plus continuity of `f.base`).
69	   - The basic-open basis (Mathlib: `PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens`
70	     transported through the affine equivalence `IsAffineOpen.fromSpec`).
71	   - The product `g := ∏ gᵢ ∈ M` lies in `appLE_unitSubmonoid` because
72	     `appLE` is a ring hom (product of units is a unit).
73	   - **Mathlib pieces** (verified iter-121/122):
74	     `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen`,
75	     `IsAffineOpen.basicOpen_eq_iff_isUnit`,
76	     `Scheme.basicOpen_appLE`.
77	
78	2. **Step 2b — cocone arm constructor**: for each `(W, hW : V ⊆
79	   f.base⁻¹W)`, build `arm_W : Γ(S, W) → Localization M` by:
80	   - Pick `g` from Step 2a with `D(g) ⊆ W ∩ U`.
81	   - Restrict `Γ(S, W) → Γ(S, S.basicOpen g)` (Mathlib:
82	     `S.presheaf.map` of the inclusion `op`).
83	   - Use `IsLocalization.Away g Γ(S, S.basicOpen g)` (Mathlib:
84	     `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen hU g`).
85	   - Apply `IsLocalization.map` for `⟨g⟩ ≤ M` (Mathlib:
86	     `IsLocalization.map (S := Γ(S, S.basicOpen g)) (T := Localization M)
87	       (Submonoid.powers_le.mpr ⟨1, by simp [g.property]⟩)`).
88	   - **Risk**: the choice of `g` depends on `W`; the arm must be
89	     well-defined (independent of the choice). This is the "Step 3
90	     side" of Step 2 — uniqueness of localization maps
91	     (`IsLocalization.lift_unique` or `IsLocalization.algHom_subsingleton`)
92	     should resolve it.
93	
94	3. **Step 2c — cocone naturality**: for each morphism
95	   `(W₁, h₁) → (W₂, h₂)` in `CostructuredArrow (Opens.map f.base).op
96	   (op V)`, verify the restriction-arm square. Use uniqueness of the
97	   localization maps (different choices of `g` for `W₁` and `W₂` give
98	   the same arm via `IsLocalization.algHom_subsingleton` /
99	   `IsLocalization.lift_unique`).
100	
101	4. **Step 2d — assemble the cocone and descend**: use
102	   `Functor.descOfIsLeftKanExtension` with `G := (Functor.const _).obj
103	   (CommRingCat.of (Localization M))` (or alternatively
104	   `IsColimit.desc` via `leftKanExtensionObjIsoColimit`). The output
105	   natural transformation, evaluated at `op V`, is the backward map.
106	
107	5. **Step 3 — inverse identities**:
108	   - `backward ∘ forward = id_{Localization M}` via
109	     `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext M` (Mathlib:
110	     `Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Defs`). Reduces to checking
111	     agreement on `algebraMap Γ(S, U) (Localization M)`, both sides
112	     send `a ↦ algebraMap a` (forward sends to `appLE_colimRingHom a`,
113	     then backward sends `algebraMap a` via the cocone arm at `op U`,
114	     which by Step 2b reduces to `a ↦ a` modulo the chosen `g`).
115	   - `forward ∘ backward = id_{A_colim}` via the `IsLeftKanExtension`
116	     `hom_ext_of_isLeftKanExtension` (or `IsColimit.hom_ext` on the
117	     cocone), reducing to checking the natural-transformation equality.

170	   a. **For Lan-functor `map_comp` rewrites** (Cluster A): use the
171	      pre-prove + `erw` idiom; do NOT use bare `rw [Functor.map_comp]`
172	      or `simp only [Functor.map_comp]`. Avoid `set` aliases inside
173	      the rewrite region. Example pattern:
174	      ```lean
175	      have hmc : ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).map (g₁ ≫ g₂) =

177	          ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).map g₂ :=

179	      erw [hmc]

183	      use `exact h_unit : IsUnit (φ.hom g)` directly (the algebra
184	      instance unifies at term level). Use `change IsUnit (φ.hom g)`
185	      when you need explicit conversion. Cite
186	      `RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra` [verified, `Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Defs:212`]
187	      for equality-manipulation contexts. Do NOT use the `have h_alg : ... := rfl; rw [h_alg]` workaround.

190	      `simpa using (adj.unit.app S.presheaf).naturality _`. The
191	      `(𝟭 _).obj` decoration is intrinsic; `Adjunction.unit_naturality`
192	      (outer version) does NOT eliminate it.

272	This is milestone M1.b of the project roadmap: the heart of the bridge between
273	the section module of `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` and the appLE-algebra
274	Kähler module on an affine chart. The proof goes via the two-direction
275	`IsLocalization.of_le` pattern (see
276	`blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex § sec:bridge`).
277	
278	The canonical `Algebra Γ(S, U) A_colim` structure is the cocone leg of the
279	directed colimit at the open `U` (valid since `V ≤ f ⁻¹ᵁ U` means
280	`fV ⊆ U`, so `U` is in the index category of the colimit). It is
281	constructed in `appLE_colimAlgebra`. -/
282	theorem appLE_isLocalization (f : X ⟶ S)
283	    {U : S.Opens} {V : X.Opens} (hU : IsAffineOpen U) (hV : IsAffineOpen V)
284	    (e : V ≤ f ⁻¹ᵁ U) :
285	    letI : Algebra Γ(S, U)
286	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (.op V)) :=
287	      appLE_colimAlgebra f e
288	    IsLocalization (appLE_unitSubmonoid f hU hV e)
289	      (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (.op V)) := by
290	  -- Strategy from blueprint § sec:bridge (M1.b proof):
291	  -- Step 0 (`isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim`): each `g ∈ M` is a unit in
292	  --   `A_colim` (via the cocone leg over `S.basicOpen g`).
293	  -- Step 1: `IsLocalization.lift` produces `A_M → A_colim`.
294	  -- Step 2: cocone universal property + basic-open refinement gives
295	  --   `A_colim → A_M` (each `Γ(S, W) → A_M` for `f V ⊆ W ⊆ U` factors through
296	  --   `Γ(S, D(g)) = (Γ(S, U))_g` for some `g ∈ M`, via quasi-compactness of
297	  --   `f V` and a basic-open cover).
298	  -- Step 3: Composites are identities via `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext` and
299	  --   `IsColimit.hom_ext` on the colimit cocone.
300	  -- Step 4: Conclude via `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` applied
301	  --   to the resulting `(Localization M) ≃ₐ[Γ(S, U)] A_colim`.
302	  letI : Algebra Γ(S, U)
303	      (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (.op V)) :=
304	    appLE_colimAlgebra f e
305	  set M := appLE_unitSubmonoid f hU hV e with hM_def
306	  -- ─────────── Step 1: forward map (Localization M) → A_colim ───────────
307	  -- Step 0 (closed iter-122) is the unit-witness ingredient.
308	  have h_unit_ring : ∀ y : M, IsUnit ((appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom y.val) := fun y =>
309	    isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim f hU hV e y.val y.property
310	  -- `forward : Localization M →+* A_colim` via `IsLocalization.lift`.
311	  let forward :
312	      Localization M →+*
313	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (.op V)) :=
314	    IsLocalization.lift (M := M) (S := Localization M) h_unit_ring
315	  -- Compatibility: forward composed with `algebraMap Γ(S, U) (Localization M)`
316	  -- equals the underlying ring map `(appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom`. This is the
317	  -- defining property of `IsLocalization.lift` (= `algebraMap Γ(S, U) A_colim`,
318	  -- by `appLE_colimAlgebra := (appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom.toAlgebra`).
319	  have h_fwd_comp :
320	      forward.comp (algebraMap Γ(S, U) (Localization M)) = (appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom :=
321	    IsLocalization.lift_comp _
322	  -- ─────────── Step 4: reduction to constructing the AlgEquiv ───────────
323	  -- Given the forward map (Step 1) plus a backward map satisfying inverse
324	  -- identities (Steps 2-3), the conclusion follows immediately via
325	  -- `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv`. We package this as a
326	  -- single residual sorry on the algebra equivalence — its construction
327	  -- is exactly the content of Steps 2-3.
328	  suffices AE :
329	      Localization M ≃ₐ[Γ(S, U)]
330	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (.op V)) from
331	    IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv M AE
332	  -- ─────────── Step 2 residual: backward map A_colim → Localization M ───────────
333	  -- The hard cofinality step that — together with Step 3's inverse identities —
334	  -- assembles into the AlgEquiv above. The cocone structure on `A_colim` is the

370	/-- The Kähler module of a localization is subsingleton.
371	This is project-local Lemma `lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton`. -/
372	theorem kaehler_localization_subsingleton
373	    {A L : Type u} [CommRing A] [CommRing L] [Algebra A L]
374	    (M : Submonoid A) [IsLocalization M L] :
375	    Subsingleton (Ω[L⁄A]) :=
376	  letI : Algebra.FormallyUnramified A L :=
377	    Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of_isLocalization (R := A) (Rₘ := L) M
378	  Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton_kaehlerDifferential
379	
380	/-- Tower-cancellation `LinearEquiv` for the Kähler module under a localization:
381	if `A → L` is a localization at `M ⊆ A` and `L → B` is a ring map forming a
382	scalar tower `A → L → B`, then the canonical map `Ω[B/A] → Ω[B/L]` is a
383	`B`-linear equivalence.
384	
385	This is project-local Lemma `lem:kaehler_quotient_localization_iso` and the most
386	extractable Mathlib contribution candidate of milestone M1 (candidate name
387	`KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified`). -/
388	noncomputable def kaehler_quotient_localization_iso
389	    {A L B : Type u} [CommRing A] [CommRing L] [CommRing B]

221	### LOW #10 — Workaround pattern for `algebraMap` non-unification
222	
223	When `letI : Algebra A B := φ.hom.toAlgebra` is in scope and the
224	goal uses `algebraMap A B`, neither `change` nor `show` will unify
225	the goal with a form involving `φ.hom` directly even though they
226	ARE definitionally equal. Workaround: introduce a `have h_alg :
227	algebraMap A B g = φ.hom g := rfl` (or via `RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra`
228	when applicable) and `rw [h_alg]`. The `rfl` form works at the
229	have-level even though `change` fails at the goal-level. Reusable
230	across the project.

222	## Knowledge Base
223	
224	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
225	
226	- **`rw [Functor.map_comp]` fails on `Lan`-defined functors; pre-prove `hmc` with `Functor.map_comp _ _ _` and use `erw [hmc]`**
227	  *(NEW iter-122 Archon canonical — surfaced during M1.b Step 0 closure)*:
228	  Goals shaped
229	  `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).map (g₁ ≫ g₂) = ...`
230	  cannot be advanced by `rw [Functor.map_comp]` (the rewriter's
231	  pattern match fails despite the printed-pattern-vs-target equality
232	  being literal). Diagnosis: the `Lan`-defined functor instance
233	  carries category-instance metadata that the rewriter's unification
234	  doesn't see through. **Workaround**: pre-prove the rewrite as a
235	  `have`, then use `erw` instead of `rw`:
236	  ```
237	  have hmc : Pf.map (g₁ ≫ g₂) = Pf.map g₁ ≫ Pf.map g₂ :=
238	    Functor.map_comp _ _ _
239	  erw [hmc]
240	  ```
241	  Companion lessons:
242	  - `rw [Category.assoc]` similarly fails on the same goals;
243	    use `exact Category.assoc _ _ _` directly when the goal
244	    is exactly the associativity equation.
245	  - `adj.unit.naturality` produces an equation involving the identity
246	    functor `((𝟭 _).obj _)`; use `simpa using
247	    ((adj.unit.app S.presheaf)).naturality _` to clean it up.
248	  **Documented at `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_122/summary.md`
249	  § Key findings.**
250	
251	- **`change`/`show` on `algebraMap g` fails even when the algebra is `φ.hom.toAlgebra`; route via `IsUnit` + `exact` instead**
252	  *(NEW iter-122 Archon canonical — surfaced during M1.b Step 0 closure)*:
253	  When `letI : Algebra A B := φ.hom.toAlgebra` is in scope and the
254	  goal involves `algebraMap A B g`, the `change` and `show` tactics
255	  fail to unify the goal with a form involving `φ.hom g` directly,
256	  even though they ARE definitionally equal (`'change' tactic
257	  failed, pattern ... is not definitionally equal to target`).
258	  **Workaround**: route the conclusion via the equation form
259	  ```
260	  have h_alg_unfold : (algebraMap A B) g = φ.hom g := rfl
261	  rw [h_alg_unfold]
262	  ```
263	  or via `RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra` when applicable. The `rfl`
264	  succeeds at the `have`-level even though `change` fails at the
265	  goal-level. For `IsUnit (algebraMap A B g)`-shaped goals,
266	  alternatively route through `IsUnit (φ.hom g)` first via a
267	  helper `have h_factor`, then `exact` the result with the
268	  defeq absorbed. Reusable across the project.
269	  **Documented at `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_122/summary.md`
270	  § Key findings.**
271	
272	- **Helper-lemma extraction pattern: name top-level decls instead of inlining `letI`-stubs to make consumers re-usable**
273	  *(NEW iter-122 Archon canonical — pattern paid off in M1 closure)*:
274	  When the plan-phase refactor introduces a sorry-bearing declaration
275	  whose body contains a `letI : Algebra A B := sorry` or similar
276	  typeclass instance, the temptation is to close the `letI` inline
277	  in the same iter. The better pattern is to lift the algebra
278	  structure out into a top-level `noncomputable def` (e.g.
279	  `appLE_colimAlgebra := (appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom.toAlgebra`),
280	  which makes the algebra re-usable across every consumer's scope.
281	  Companion: when the proof itself decomposes into "step 0 sub-claim
282	  + steps 1–N main", extract step 0 as a named helper theorem
283	  (e.g. `isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim`) before the main proof
284	  body; the named helper is then directly invokable as a hypothesis
285	  to `IsLocalization.lift` (Step 1) and similar consumers in the
286	  same iter or downstream iters. The iter-122 M1 closure used this
287	  pattern with `appLE_colimRingHom`, `appLE_colimAlgebra`,
288	  `appLE_colimRingHom_comp_φV`, `isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim`
289	  — making the bridge body `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_equiv_kaehler_appLE`
290	  close in a single Edit after the helpers landed. Reusable across
291	  the project (cf. the iter-098 split-slot pattern with
292	  `alternating_sum_pi_smul_aux_sum_comp`).
293	
294	- **`Functor.Final` is the WRONG framing for "directed colimit of localizations = localization at the union submonoid"; use `IsLocalization.of_le` with cocone universality**
295	  *(NEW iter-121 Archon canonical — surfaced by `mathlib-analogist-bridge-iter121`)*:
296	  the natural-looking colim-comparison shape
297	  ```
298	  colim_{g ∈ M, in the basic-open-cocone} A_g  ≃  Localization M A
299	  ```
300	  has no off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma in `b80f227` (verified via
301	  `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, `lean_local_search` on multiple
302	  variants). Trying to use `CategoryTheory.Functor.Final` to reduce
303	  to a cofinality-of-indexing-category argument leads into Mathlib
304	  territory that doesn't exist. The cleanly Mathlib-aligned path is
305	  to **bypass the colim-comparison entirely** and prove
306	  `IsLocalization M A_colim` directly:
307	  (a) Construct `A_M → A_colim` via `Localization M`'s universal
308	      property (after showing each `g ∈ M` is a unit in `A_colim`).
309	  (b) Construct `A_colim → A_M` via the colim cocone's universal
310	      property, picking basic-open refinements `D(g) ⊆ W` on each
311	      open and mapping `Γ(S, W) → Γ(S, D(g)) = A_g → A_M`.
312	  (c) Verify the composites are identity via
313	      `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext`.
314	  (d) Conclude `IsLocalization M A_colim` via `IsLocalization.of_le`
315	      (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Defs`:
316	      `[IsLocalization M S] → (h : ∀ n ∈ N, IsUnit (algebraMap n)) → IsLocalization N S`).
317	  Reusable for any "directed colimit of localizations = localization
318	  at union" claim in Lean. **Documented at
319	  `analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md` and
320	  `STRATEGY.md § Roadmap M1` (iter-121).**
321	
322	- **`KaehlerDifferential` of a localization is `Subsingleton` via `FormallyUnramified.of_isLocalization` — NOT a Mathlib gap**
323	  *(NEW iter-121 Archon canonical — surfaced by `mathlib-analogist-bridge-iter121`)*:
324	  the claim "`Ω[L⁄A]` is subsingleton when `A → L` is a localization"
325	  is NOT a Mathlib gap, contrary to the iter-121 first-draft blueprint.
326	  Mathlib supplies it as the 2-line composition:
327	  ```
328	  [IsLocalization M L]
329	    → Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of_isLocalization M
330	         (Mathlib.RingTheory.Unramified.Basic:303)
331	    → @Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton_kaehlerDifferential _ _ _ _ _ _
332	         (Mathlib.RingTheory.Unramified.Basic:57-59, instance)
333	  ```
334	  Use this whenever a proof needs `Ω[L/A] = 0` from a localization
335	  hypothesis; do NOT carry it around as a standalone `\lean{...}`-tagged
336	  declaration. The corollary "Kähler module of a quotient-localization
337	  iso" (`tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale`-style: `Ω[B/A] ≃ₗ[B] Ω[B/L]`
338	  for `L = Localization M A`) is ~10–30 LOC via
339	  `KaehlerDifferential.exact_mapBaseChange_map` +
340	  `KaehlerDifferential.map_surjective`, and is the most extractable
341	  Mathlib contribution candidate from M1 (generalises
342	  `tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale` to "only the lower step is
343	  formally unramified"). **Documented at
344	  `analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md` and
345	  `STRATEGY.md § Roadmap M1` (iter-121).**
346	
347	- **Proactive `mathlib-analogist` dispatch BEFORE the bridge ships pays for itself in saved refactor cost**
348	  *(NEW iter-121 Archon canonical)*: dispatching `mathlib-analogist`
349	  proactively against a not-yet-shipped infrastructure declaration
350	  (the M1 bridge `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_equiv_kaehler_appLE`)
351	  returned 5 `ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB` + 1 `NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL`
352	  findings (LinearEquiv shape with `@[simps]`; bare-theorem
353	  `IsLocalization` packaging; `_iso_` → `_equiv_` rename; namespace
354	  `IsAffineOpen.…` over `Scheme.…`; M1.c is not a Mathlib gap; M1.b
355	  cofinality re-framing). Each of these would have cost a refactor +
356	  blueprint-writer + prover-rework round if discovered post-ship;
357	  caught pre-ship, they cost only a directive update. **Pattern**:
358	  any time the loop is about to introduce a new sorry-bearing
359	  declaration tied to a Mathlib namespace the project hasn't authored
360	  before, dispatch `mathlib-analogist` BEFORE the declaration enters
361	  the Lean tree. Persistent file
362	  `analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md` is the
363	  artefact for future iters. Caveat surfaced this iter: do NOT
364	  parallel-dispatch the analogist with a `blueprint-writer` on the
365	  SAME chapter — the writer can't see the analogist's findings yet,
366	  so it lands sub-optimal vocabulary that the next iter has to
367	  correct.
368	
369	- **`by_cases h : genus C = 0` is a well-formed Lean decomposition for `nonempty_jacobianWitness`-style protected signatures with no genus parameter**
370	  *(NEW iter-121 Archon canonical — strategy-critic-iter121 alternative #1 adopted)*:
371	  when a protected signature quantifies over a `Nat`-genus-implicit
372	  object (here: `C : Over (Spec (.of k))` with smoothness +
373	  irreducibility + properness instances but no `g : ℕ` parameter),
374	  the body may still be a `Nat`-valued case split because
375	  `AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℕ` is `DecidableEq`. The shape
376	  ```
377	  theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness ... := by
378	    by_cases h : AlgebraicGeometry.genus (k := k) C.left = 0
379	    · exact ⟨genusZeroWitness C h⟩      -- closed by M2

380	    · exact ⟨positiveGenusWitness C (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero h)⟩  -- closed by M3
381	  ```
382	  converts a "genus-stratified" prose proof into an explicit
383	  Lean-level decomposition, which the autonomous loop can then close
384	  one arm at a time. Reusable for any all-quantified protected
385	  signature whose informal proof case-splits on a decidable scalar.
386	  **Documented at `STRATEGY.md § Decomposition: genus-stratified
387	  body of nonempty_jacobianWitness` (iter-121).**

389	- **Genus-0 over non-algebraically-closed `k` is NOT `C ≅ ℙ¹_k`; use base change to `k̄` + Galois descent**
390	  *(NEW iter-121 Archon canonical — strategy-critic-iter121 alternative #2 adopted)*:
391	  the iter-121 first-draft strategy claimed M2.c was the
392	  identification `C ≅ ℙ¹_k` for genus-0 curves. This is
393	  **mathematically false** on the protected
394	  `nonempty_jacobianWitness` signature, which permits curves
395	  without `k`-rational points: Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ`
396	  (`x² + y² + z² = 0` is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
397	  curve of genus 0 with no `ℚ`-point and is NOT isomorphic to
398	  `ℙ¹_ℚ`). The corrected route is: base change to `k̄` (where
399	  `C_{k̄}` is automatically nonempty geometrically integral, hence
400	  has a `k̄`-point and IS isomorphic to `ℙ¹_{k̄}`); apply rigidity
401	  for `ℙ¹_{k̄} → A_{k̄}`; Galois-descend constancy back to `k`.
402	  Reusable for any "X ≅ ℙ¹_k under genus-0 + smooth + proper +
403	  geometrically irreducible" claim — the `k`-rational-point
404	  hypothesis is silently required and is NOT implied by the named
405	  hypotheses. **Documented at `STRATEGY.md § Roadmap M2` and
406	  `Jacobian.tex § (C.2)` 7-sub-step expansion (iter-121).**
407	
408	- **Presheaf-of-modules section at a single open is NOT defeq to the chart-local Mathlib ring-theoretic output — unfold before claiming `rfl`**
409	  *(NEW iter-119 Archon canonical)*: a blueprint claim that
410	  `(relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj (.op V₀)` is
411	  "definitionally equal" to `Ω[Γ(X,V₀)⁄Γ(S,U₀)]` via the project-local
412	  `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler` (whose body is `rfl`) is
413	  **WRONG**. The `rfl` body identifies the section module with
414	  `CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op V₀))` where the source
415	  ring of `φ' := ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction
416	  CommRingCat f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm f.c` is
417	  `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj
418	  (.op V₀)` — i.e. the inverse-image presheaf at `V₀`, which by the
419	  left Kan extension formula computes to
420	  `colim_{f V₀ ⊆ W ⊆ S} S.presheaf.obj W`. This is a colimit ring
421	  strictly larger than `Γ(S, U₀)` in general; the two Kähler
422	  differential modules are not definitionally the same type, and the
423	  bridge requires a real `LinearEquiv` constructed via cofinality of
424	  `U₀` in the colimit cone of neighbourhoods of `f V₀` conditional on
425	  `IsAffineOpen U₀ ∧ IsAffineOpen V₀ ∧ V₀ ≤ f⁻¹ᵁ U₀`. **Pattern**: when
426	  a blueprint claims "definitional equality" between a presheaf-of-
427	  modules construction and a ring-theoretic Mathlib output, *unfold the
428	  construction at the Lean level before accepting the claim*. The left
429	  Kan extension at a single open is generically not `rfl`-equal to the
430	  ring-of-sections-on-a-containing-open. **Documented at
431	  `Differentials.lean:130-134` (iter-119 prover Step-5 comment block)
432	  and `Differentials.tex:91-104` (`% NOTE (iter-119 prover finding)`
433	  block added this review iter); iter-120 corrective is a new helper
434	  `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_iso_kaehler_appLE`.**

150	
151	  \emph{M1.e: combine with the $\mathrm{rfl}$-identification of the presheaf section.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}, $(\mathrm{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}\,f).\mathrm{presheaf}(V) = \Omega_{B / A_{\mathrm{colim}}}$ definitionally. Composing the rfl-equality with the inverse of the M1.d iso gives the claimed $B$-linear isomorphism.
152	\end{proof}
153	
154	\begin{lemma}\leanok
155	[$A \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ is a localization at $M$]
156	  \label{lem:appLE_isLocalization}
157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.appLE_isLocalization}
158	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
159	  In the setting of Theorem~\ref{thm:relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_equiv_kaehler_appLE}, the canonical ring homomorphism $A \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ exhibits $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ as the localization of $A$ at the multiplicative set $M := \{g \in A : (f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}(g) \in B^{\times}\}$.
160	\end{lemma}
161	
162	\begin{proof}
163	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
164	  \leanok
165	  We construct the \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ predicate directly via the two-direction \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le} pattern, avoiding any \texttt{Functor.Final} colim-comparison (Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no off-the-shelf ``colim of localizations is localization at the union submonoid'' lemma, so this pattern is the canonical project-local route).
166	
167	  \emph{Step 0: each $g \in M$ is a unit in $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} Fix $g \in M$, so $\mathrm{appLE}(g) \in B^{\times}$. The basic open $D(g) \subseteq U$ contains $f V$ (by the bridge identity $f V \subseteq D(g) \iff \mathrm{appLE}(g) \in B^{\times}$, following from \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}'s \texttt{IsAffineOpen.basicOpen\_eq\_iff\_isUnit} together with $D(g) = f^{-1}\,U.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g$ on the affine source side). The cocone leg of the directed colimit corresponding to the open $D(g)$ produces a ring map $\struct{S}(D(g)) \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$, and on $D(g)$ itself the element $g$ is invertible (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen} supplies the \texttt{IsLocalization.Away}~$g$~$\struct{S}(D(g))$ instance, so $g$ is a unit in $\struct{S}(D(g))$). Pushing this unit through the cocone leg shows $g$ is a unit in $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.
168	
169	  \emph{Step 1: the algebra map $A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} The universal property of \texttt{Localization}~$M$ (\texttt{IsLocalization.lift}, \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic}) applied to the algebra map $A \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ — together with Step 0 — produces a ring homomorphism $\varphi \colon A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ over $A$.
170	
171	  \emph{Step 2: the algebra map $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M$.} The colimit $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ has the universal property of the cocone over $\{\struct{S}(W) : f V \subseteq W \subseteq S\}$; to produce a ring map out of $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ it suffices to specify a compatible family of ring maps from each cocone node. For each open $W \supseteq f V$, restrict to $W \subseteq U$ (intersecting with $U$ preserves the cocone since $f V \subseteq U$), cover $W \cap U$ by basic opens $D(g_i) \subseteq U$ (\texttt{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}), use quasi-compactness of $f V$ to extract a finite sub-cover, take $g := \prod g_i$ so that $D(g) \subseteq W \cap U$ and $f V \subseteq D(g)$ (i.e.\ $g \in M$); then $\struct{S}(W) \to \struct{S}(D(g)) = A_g \to A_M$ via the canonical $A_g \to A_M$ supplied by the inclusion of submonoids $\langle g \rangle \subseteq M$. Compatibility on intersections follows from the universal property of $A_g$ at each level.
172	
173	  \emph{Step 3: composites are identities.} \texttt{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic}) shows that any two ring homomorphisms $A_M \to R$ agreeing on the image of $A$ are equal. The composite $A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M$ agrees with the identity on $A$ (both send $a \mapsto a/1$), hence is the identity. Symmetrically, any two ring homomorphisms $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to R$ agreeing on every cocone node $\struct{S}(W)$ are equal; the composite $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ agrees with the identity on every $\struct{S}(D(g))$ for $g \in M$ (both factor through the canonical $\struct{S}(D(g)) \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$), and basic opens with $g \in M$ form a cofinal subfamily of opens containing $f V$, so the composite is the identity on the whole colimit.
174	
175	  \emph{Step 4: conclude \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} The isomorphism $A_M \cong A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ over $A$ — together with the canonical \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_M$ instance for \texttt{Localization}~$M$ — implies \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ via \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le}~\texttt{[verified]} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Defs}), more directly via \texttt{IsLocalization.isLocalization\_of\_algEquiv} or \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_ringEquiv}.
176	
177	  \emph{Mathlib leverage summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces are
178	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen} (Steps 0, 2),
179	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.basicOpen\_eq\_iff\_isUnit} (Step 0 bridge identity),
180	  \texttt{IsLocalization.lift} (Step 1),
181	  \texttt{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext} (Step 3), and
182	  \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le} or \texttt{IsLocalization.isLocalization\_of\_algEquiv} (Step 4).
183	  The cocone leg-morphisms of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} are verified to exist in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}.
184	
185	  \emph{Cross-chapter parallel.} A similar two-direction \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le} pattern can be used elsewhere in the project whenever a colimit of localizations needs to be identified with a localization at the union submonoid.
186	
187	  The Mathlib analogist's persistent file \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md} (iter-121) records the full design rationale for the chosen API shape.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[K\"ahler module of a localization is trivial]
192	  \label{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
194	  \uses{lem:appLE_isLocalization}
195	  Let $A \to L$ be a localization at a multiplicative subset $M \subseteq A$. Then $\Omega_{L / A}$ is subsingleton (the zero $L$-module).
196	\end{lemma}
197	
198	\begin{proof}
199	
200	\leanok
201	  This is a thin re-export of Mathlib's existing infrastructure: \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~\texttt{[verified]} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Unramified.Basic}, line 303) produces the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified}~$A$~$L$ from any \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$L$ predicate; the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton\_kaehlerDifferential}~\texttt{[verified]} (same file, lines 57--59) then yields \texttt{Subsingleton}~$(\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential}~A~L)$. Hence the project-side lemma is a two-line re-export ($\mathtt{haveI} := \mathtt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~M$ \texttt{; infer\_instance}); contra an earlier framing, no Mathlib gap-fill is required for this lemma.
202	\end{proof}
203	
204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
205	[Tower-cancellation equivalence for K\"ahler modules under localization]
206	  \label{lem:kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
208	  \uses{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
209	  Let $A \to L$ be a localization (in the sense of \texttt{IsLocalization}), and let $L \to B$ be a ring map forming a scalar tower $A \to L \to B$. Then the canonical $B$-linear map $\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/L}$ induced by the tower is a $B$-linear equivalence.
210	\end{lemma}
211	
212	\begin{proof}
213	  \uses{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
214	  \leanok
215	  The second fundamental exact sequence of K\"ahler differentials reads
216	  \[
217	    B \otimes_{L} \Omega_{L/A} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/A} \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/L} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
218	  \]
219	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}, the left term vanishes (since $\Omega_{L/A}$ is subsingleton and tensoring against it gives the zero module). Therefore the right surjection has zero kernel and is a $B$-linear equivalence. The Mathlib closure pieces are \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map}~\texttt{[verified]} (the exact sequence above) and \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map\_surjective}~\texttt{[verified]} (the right-hand surjection), both in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}; the project-side declaration packages these into a \texttt{LinearEquiv} via \texttt{LinearEquiv.ofBijective}. This is the most extractable Mathlib contribution candidate of milestone~M1: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case, candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}.
220	\end{proof}
221	
222	\begin{remark}[Mathlib contribution candidate]
223	  \label{rem:bridge_mathlib_pr}
224	  Per the analogist's analysis (\texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}, iter-121), the most extractable Mathlib contribution candidate of milestone~M1 is Lemma~\ref{lem:kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case (candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}, candidate Mathlib home \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}). Lemma~\ref{lem:appLE_isLocalization} is more scheme-morphism-shaped and is best kept project-local; if a future Mathlib PR wants it, extraction from \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} is straightforward.
225	\end{remark}
226	
227	\section{Converse direction (milestone M4)}
228	\label{sec:converse-out-of-scope}
229	
230	This direction is recorded as an optional future milestone~M4 (see \texttt{STRATEGY.md}). Closure requires the deformation-theoretic hypothesis \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~$A$~$B$\texttt{)}, which the project may wire up if a downstream consumer demands the iff form. The remainder of this section documents the obstruction in concrete form: a counterexample showing that without that hypothesis the converse fails, and a comparison with the Mathlib converse lemma at the ring level.
231	
232	The reverse implication
233	\[
234	  \forall x\in X,\ \exists\,U\text{ affine},\ \Omega_{X/S}(U)\text{ free of rank }n
235	  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f \text{ smooth of relative dimension } n
236	\]
237	is \emph{mathematically false} as stated, even under the auxiliary hypothesis that $f$ is locally of finite presentation. Local freeness of $\Omega$ alone does not imply flatness, and flatness (equivalently, formal smoothness via vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology) is genuine deformation-theoretic content not encoded in the section-module hypothesis. This is why the iff form of the criterion present in earlier iterations was demoted to a forward implication.
238	
239	\begin{remark}[Explicit counterexample]

# lean-vs-blueprint-checker — Differentials.lean vs Differentials.tex (iter-123 review)

## Scope

Single-file bidirectional check:

- **Lean file**: `/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean`
- **Blueprint chapter**: `/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`

## Why this file

This is the only `.lean` file that received prover edits this iter (the iter-123 M1.b prover lane on `appLE_isLocalization`). The prover landed Steps 1 and 4 of the blueprint's 4-step `IsLocalization.of_le` plan inside the body of `appLE_isLocalization` and packaged Steps 2-3 into a single residual `sorry` on an `AlgEquiv` between `Localization M` and `A_colim` (suffices block at L328-L362).

## What to check

Bidirectional:

1. **Lean → blueprint**: does every fully-proved Lean declaration in `Differentials.lean` correspond to a `\lean{...}`-tagged statement block in `Differentials.tex`? Specifically:
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.appLE_isLocalization` (L282; body L304-L362; still 1 sorry residual)
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim` (Step 0 helper, fully proved iter-122)
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_localization_subsingleton`
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_quotient_localization_iso`
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_equiv_kaehler_appLE`
   - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega` (closed iter-120)

2. **Blueprint → Lean**: does the `\lean{...}` macro on each lemma in the chapter point at an existing Lean declaration in the file? Are the prose proofs in the chapter (Steps 0-4 of `lem:appLE_isLocalization`) accurate to the Lean body — in particular Step 4 specifically uses `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` (the planner verified that an `AlgEquiv` is required, not a `RingEquiv` — make sure the chapter's "or via `IsLocalization.of_le` ... or `IsLocalization.of_ringEquiv`" hedge at L175 is not misleading).

3. **Statement-level signature alignment**: the chapter's M1.b proof prose (§ sec:bridge, Lemma lem:appLE_isLocalization Step 0) writes "first observe that each $g \in M$ is a unit in $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$." This is now broken out as a named helper `isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim` (top-level theorem; iter-122 closure). Is this naming mismatch flagged in the chapter, or does the chapter still read as if Step 0 were inlined? If the latter, this is a documentation-drift finding — recommend adding a `\lean{...isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim}` sub-block.

4. **Open questions for the planner**: the residual sorry at L362 is the `AlgEquiv` packaging Step 2 + Step 3 + the algebra-map compatibility. The blueprint's Step 2 prose at L171 describes the cofinality argument via a basic-open refinement; the blueprint's Step 3 prose at L173 describes the inverse-identity verification. Are these adequately specified for the next prover iter, or does the blueprint need expansion on the `IsLocalization.map` / `IsColimit.desc` plumbing? Flag must-fix-this-iter only if the chapter is genuinely thin enough to mislead the iter-124 prover.

## Output

Report at `task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-differentials-review123.md`. Use the standard bidirectional-check format: per-axis findings (Lean→blueprint, blueprint→Lean, signature alignment), per-finding severity (must-fix / major / minor / cosmetic), suggested action per finding.

160	\end{lemma}

162	\begin{proof}
163	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
164	  \leanok
165	  We construct the \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ predicate directly via the two-direction \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le} pattern, avoiding any \texttt{Functor.Final} colim-comparison (Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no off-the-shelf ``colim of localizations is localization at the union submonoid'' lemma, so this pattern is the canonical project-local route).
166	
167	  \emph{Step 0: each $g \in M$ is a unit in $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} Fix $g \in M$, so $\mathrm{appLE}(g) \in B^{\times}$. The basic open $D(g) \subseteq U$ contains $f V$ (by the bridge identity $f V \subseteq D(g) \iff \mathrm{appLE}(g) \in B^{\times}$, following from \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}'s \texttt{IsAffineOpen.basicOpen\_eq\_iff\_isUnit} together with $D(g) = f^{-1}\,U.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g$ on the affine source side). The cocone leg of the directed colimit corresponding to the open $D(g)$ produces a ring map $\struct{S}(D(g)) \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$, and on $D(g)$ itself the element $g$ is invertible (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen} supplies the \texttt{IsLocalization.Away}~$g$~$\struct{S}(D(g))$ instance, so $g$ is a unit in $\struct{S}(D(g))$). Pushing this unit through the cocone leg shows $g$ is a unit in $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.
168	
169	  \emph{Step 1: the algebra map $A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} The universal property of \texttt{Localization}~$M$ (\texttt{IsLocalization.lift}, \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic}) applied to the algebra map $A \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ — together with Step 0 — produces a ring homomorphism $\varphi \colon A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ over $A$.
170	
171	  \emph{Step 2: the algebra map $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M$.} The colimit $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ has the universal property of the cocone over $\{\struct{S}(W) : f V \subseteq W \subseteq S\}$; to produce a ring map out of $A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ it suffices to specify a compatible family of ring maps from each cocone node. For each open $W \supseteq f V$, restrict to $W \subseteq U$ (intersecting with $U$ preserves the cocone since $f V \subseteq U$), cover $W \cap U$ by basic opens $D(g_i) \subseteq U$ (\texttt{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}), use quasi-compactness of $f V$ to extract a finite sub-cover, take $g := \prod g_i$ so that $D(g) \subseteq W \cap U$ and $f V \subseteq D(g)$ (i.e.\ $g \in M$); then $\struct{S}(W) \to \struct{S}(D(g)) = A_g \to A_M$ via the canonical $A_g \to A_M$ supplied by the inclusion of submonoids $\langle g \rangle \subseteq M$. Compatibility on intersections follows from the universal property of $A_g$ at each level.
172	
173	  \emph{Step 3: composites are identities.} \texttt{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic}) shows that any two ring homomorphisms $A_M \to R$ agreeing on the image of $A$ are equal. The composite $A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M$ agrees with the identity on $A$ (both send $a \mapsto a/1$), hence is the identity. Symmetrically, any two ring homomorphisms $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to R$ agreeing on every cocone node $\struct{S}(W)$ are equal; the composite $A_{\mathrm{colim}} \to A_M \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ agrees with the identity on every $\struct{S}(D(g))$ for $g \in M$ (both factor through the canonical $\struct{S}(D(g)) \to A_{\mathrm{colim}}$), and basic opens with $g \in M$ form a cofinal subfamily of opens containing $f V$, so the composite is the identity on the whole colimit.
174	
175	  \emph{Step 4: conclude \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$.} The isomorphism $A_M \cong A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ over $A$ — together with the canonical \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_M$ instance for \texttt{Localization}~$M$ — implies \texttt{IsLocalization}~$M$~$A_{\mathrm{colim}}$ via \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le}~\texttt{[verified]} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Defs}), more directly via \texttt{IsLocalization.isLocalization\_of\_algEquiv} or \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_ringEquiv}.
176	
177	  \emph{Mathlib leverage summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces are
178	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen} (Steps 0, 2),
179	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.basicOpen\_eq\_iff\_isUnit} (Step 0 bridge identity),
180	  \texttt{IsLocalization.lift} (Step 1),
181	  \texttt{IsLocalization.ringHom\_ext} (Step 3), and
182	  \texttt{IsLocalization.of\_le} or \texttt{IsLocalization.isLocalization\_of\_algEquiv} (Step 4).
183	  The cocone leg-morphisms of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} are verified to exist in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}.

## Key findings / proof patterns discovered

### Pattern: `suffices` reduction to consolidate multi-step inverse-construction PARTIALs into a single residual sorry

When a 4-step proof has steps 1 + 4 immediately constructible and
steps 2 + 3 jointly required to build a single intermediate
existential (e.g. an isomorphism, an `AlgEquiv`, a `LinearEquiv`),
the right shape is:

```lean
-- Step 1: build the forward direction (concrete code)
have forward_data : ... := ...
-- Step 4: reduce to the existential via a Mathlib constructor
suffices intermediate : SomeIsoType from
  Mathlib.someClosingLemma forward_data intermediate
-- Step 2 + Step 3: the residual (single sorry here)
sorry
```

This keeps the file's sorry count at exactly 1 (vs. splitting the
residual into separate sorries for backward map / inverse 1 / inverse
2 / algebra-map compatibility), AND makes the iter-N+1 prover's
target explicitly the intermediate object (not a fragmented list
of obligations). Reusable across the project; companion pattern
to iter-119's "constructurally-minimal residual-sorry cascade"
(`constructor / all_goals first | exact _ | sorry`).

Iter-123 instance: at `Differentials.lean:328-331`, the body
constructs the forward map (Step 1) + reduces via
`IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` (Step 4); the
remaining content is exactly the `Localization M ≃ₐ[Γ(S, U)]
A_colim` AlgEquiv, packaged as a single sorry.

### Pattern: `IsLocalization.lift_comp` captures the compatibility witness

When using `IsLocalization.lift (M := M) (S := Localization M) hg` to
build a ring map `Localization M →+* B` from a unit-of-image witness
`hg : ∀ y : M, IsUnit (g y.val)`, the compatibility equation
`(lift hg).comp (algebraMap A (Localization M)) = g` is provided
directly by `IsLocalization.lift_comp`. Use this to package the
"forward map composed with `algebraMap` equals the underlying ring
map" fact for downstream Steps 3 (inverse-identity verification)
and 4 (algebra-map compatibility for `AlgEquiv.ofRingEquiv`).

Iter-123 instance: `h_fwd_comp` at `Differentials.lean:319-321`.

### Pattern: `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` is the canonical Step-4 constructor

The iter-122 plan-phase recommendation MEDIUM/HIGH listed
`IsLocalization.of_le` as the Step-4 closer; the iter-123 M1.b
tactical analogist re-verified and confirmed that
`IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv M : (Localization M
≃ₐ[A] B) → IsLocalization M B` is the canonical Mathlib constructor
for the "package the AlgEquiv → IsLocalization" reduction (takes
an `AlgEquiv`, not a `RingEquiv`; `Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic:304`).
The "ringEquiv-only" alternative is
`IsLocalization.isLocalization_iff_of_ringEquiv` which is an iff
shape (less direct to use).

- **Step 2 is genuinely novel**: the project has never previously
  used `Functor.descOfIsLeftKanExtension` or `IsColimit.desc` for
  scheme-theoretic cofinality. The iter-123 mathlib-analogist's
  Cluster A (Lan `map_comp` workaround) + Cluster D (unit.naturality
  via `simpa`) playbook is the right tactical toolkit; the iter-122

- **Step 2 may benefit from refactor-extraction**: if iter-124 stalls
  on Step 2's cocone-arm construction or the basic-open-cover
  helper, the iter-122 recommendations MEDIUM #6 plan still applies:
  extract a top-level `colim_descend_via_basic_open_refinement`
  (or similar) named helper before the prover lane. Explicit
  signatures avoid the rewriter pitfalls that bloated iter-122
  Step 0 work to ~15 attempts.
- **Step 3 reduces to an `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext` plus an
  `IsColimit.hom_ext` / `IsLeftKanExtension.hom_ext_of_isLeftKanExtension`**:
  both Mathlib closure pieces are verified iter-122/123. The
  `appLE_colimRingHom_comp_φV` factorisation theorem (L116, fully

`suffices AE : Localization M ≃ₐ[Γ(S, U)] A_colim` block).

**Recommended action**: dispatch a prover on `Differentials.lean`
with PROGRESS.md objectives targeting the AlgEquiv construction.
Per the prover task result § "Detailed plan for iter-124", the
residual decomposes into 6 sub-steps (~140-230 LOC total):

- **Step 2a (~30–60 LOC)**: basic-open-cover helper. For every
  `W : Opens S` with `fV ⊆ W`, prove there exists `g ∈ M` with
  `S.basicOpen g ⊆ W ∩ U`. Inputs (all verified iter-121/122):
  `IsAffineOpen.isCompact` (quasi-compactness of `fV`),
  `PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens` (basis transport),
  `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen`,
  `IsAffineOpen.basicOpen_eq_iff_isUnit`, `Scheme.basicOpen_appLE`,
  plus the fact that products of units are units (the appLE map is
  a ring homomorphism).

- **Step 2b (~30–50 LOC)**: cocone arm constructor. For each
  `(W, hW : V ⊆ f⁻¹ᵁ W)`, build `arm_W : Γ(S, W) → Localization M`
  via: pick `g` from Step 2a, restrict `Γ(S, W) → Γ(S, S.basicOpen g)`,
  use `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen` to identify the latter
  with `(Γ(S, U))_g`, then apply `IsLocalization.map` for
  `⟨g⟩ ≤ M`. Risk: the choice of `g` depends on `W`, so the arm
  must be well-defined (independent of `g`); resolve via
  `IsLocalization.lift_unique` or `IsLocalization.algHom_subsingleton`.

- **Step 2c (~30–50 LOC)**: cocone naturality. For each morphism
  `(W₁, h₁) → (W₂, h₂)` in `CostructuredArrow (Opens.map f.base).op (op V)`,
  verify the restriction-arm square via `IsLocalization.algHom_subsingleton`.

- **Step 2d (~10–20 LOC)**: assemble the cocone and descend via
  `Functor.descOfIsLeftKanExtension` (with `G := (Functor.const _).obj
  (CommRingCat.of (Localization M))`) — or alternatively `IsColimit.desc`
  via `leftKanExtensionObjIsoColimit`. Evaluated at `op V`, the output
  is the backward map `A_colim → Localization M`.

- **Step 3 (~30–50 LOC)**: inverse identities.
  - `backward ∘ forward = id_{Localization M}` via
    `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext M` — reduces to checking agreement on
    `algebraMap Γ(S, U) (Localization M)`. Both sides send
    `a ↦ algebraMap a` (forward sends to `(appLE_colimRingHom f e).hom a`;
    backward sends via the cocone arm at `op U`, which by Step 2b
    reduces to `a ↦ a` modulo the chosen `g`).
  - `forward ∘ backward = id_{A_colim}` via
    `IsLeftKanExtension.hom_ext_of_isLeftKanExtension` (or
    `IsColimit.hom_ext` on the cocone). Reduces to checking the
    natural-transformation equality at each cocone arm.

- **Step 4 — already in place**: `AlgEquiv.ofRingEquiv` on
  `RingEquiv.ofRingHom forward backward h_fb h_bf`, with the
  algebra-map compatibility witness via `IsLocalization.lift_eq`
  (the recipe sketch is at `Differentials.lean:348-361` comments
  block).

**Tactical playbook** (from iter-123 mathlib-analogist; already applied
iter-122/123 and reusable):
- **Cluster A (Lan `map_comp`)**: pre-prove + `erw` idiom; avoid `set`
  aliases inside the rewrite region. Already used at L234-239 in
  `isUnit_appLE_unitSubmonoid_in_colim`; will recur in Step 2c naturality.
- **Cluster B (IsLocalization shape)**: Step 4 uses
  `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` — already in place.
- **Cluster C (algebraMap-from-`.toAlgebra`)**: use direct equality
  or `RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra` for explicit conversion; avoid
  `change`/`show` on `algebraMap`.
- **Cluster D (unit.naturality cleanup)**: `simpa using (adj.unit.app
  S.presheaf).naturality _` for inner `(𝟭 _).obj` decorations.

breakdown allows independent sub-progress (e.g. closing Step 2a as a
standalone helper without needing the full backward map).

prior project work uses scheme-theoretic `Functor.descOfIsLeftKanExtension`
or `IsColimit.desc`). If iter-124 stalls on Step 2's cofinality
work — concretely, more than 2 PARTIAL outcomes — fire the STRATEGY.md

commitment.

## Overall progress (this session detail)

- **Total active syntactic sorry sites**: **2**, distributed:

## What the iter-123 prover got right

- **Right Mathlib closer for Step 4**. The iter-123 plan-phase

  `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` (takes `AlgEquiv`,
  not `RingEquiv`) — the iter-122 plan listed `IsLocalization.of_le`
  which is a different constructor (and would have required a
  separate witness shape). The iter-123 prover picked the verified
  one, used `suffices` to reduce the goal, and the closure landed in

  body contains exactly **one** residual sorry — the AlgEquiv — not

  count; the prover correctly read PROGRESS.md's COMPLETE/PARTIAL

### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)

input.new_string:
## Knowledge Base

### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)

- **`suffices` reduction to consolidate multi-step inverse-construction PARTIALs into a single residual sorry**
  *(NEW iter-123 Archon canonical — surfaced during M1.b Step 1 + Step 4 closure on `appLE_isLocalization`)*:
  When a multi-step proof has steps 1 + N immediately constructible
  and intermediate steps jointly required to build a single object
  (an iso, an `AlgEquiv`, a `LinearEquiv`), use `suffices` to reduce
  to that object. This keeps the file's sorry count at exactly 1
  (vs. splitting the residual into separate sorries for each
  remaining step), AND makes the iter-N+1 prover's target explicitly
  the intermediate object. Companion to iter-119's "structurally-
  minimal residual-sorry cascade" (`constructor / all_goals first | exact _ | sorry`).
  ```lean
  -- Step 1: build the forward direction (concrete code).
  have forward_data : ... := ...
  -- Step N: reduce to the existential via a Mathlib constructor.
  suffices intermediate : SomeIsoType from
    Mathlib.someClosingLemma forward_data intermediate
  -- Steps 2 … N-1: the residual (single sorry here).
  sorry
  ```
  Iter-123 instance: `Differentials.lean:328–331` reduces
  `IsLocalization M A_colim` via
  `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv M AE`
  to the single residual `AlgEquiv` `Localization M ≃ₐ[Γ(S, U)] A_colim`.
  Companion: `IsLocalization.lift_comp` captures the compatibility
  witness `(IsLocalization.lift hg).comp algebraMap = g` for free
  — use it to package the forward-map-composed-with-algebra-map
  fact for downstream Step-3 inverse-identity verification and
  Step-4 algebra-map compatibility. **Documented at
  `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_123/summary.md` § Key findings**.

- **`IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv` (not `_of_le` or `_of_ringEquiv`) is the canonical Step-4 closer for "colim of localizations" → `IsLocalization`**
  *(NEW iter-123 Archon canonical — verified by iter-123 M1.b tactical mathlib-analogist)*:
  When proving `IsLocalization M B` via a constructed AlgEquiv
  `Localization M ≃ₐ[A] B` (the cleanest output of the two-direction
  forward/backward construction documented in the iter-121 Knowledge
  Base entry), the right Mathlib closer is
  `IsLocalization.isLocalization_of_algEquiv M : (Localization M ≃ₐ[A] B) → IsLocalization M B`
  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Basic:304`).
  The iter-122 plan-phase recommendation listed `IsLocalization.of_le` —
  that lemma is for the *different* shape "extend an existing `IsLocalization
  M S` to `IsLocalization N S` for `M ≤ N`" — and is NOT directly applicable
  when only an iso is in hand. The "`isLocalization_iff_of_ringEquiv`"
  alternative is iff-shaped (less direct). The constructor requires an
  `AlgEquiv` (not a `RingEquiv`); package via `AlgEquiv.ofRingEquiv` from
  a `RingEquiv` + algebra-map compatibility (the iter-123
  `Differentials.lean:348–361` recipe sketch documents the assembly).
  **Documented at `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_123/summary.md` § Key findings**.

- **`rw [Functor.map_comp]` fails on `Lan`-defined functors; pre-prove `hmc` with `Functor.map_comp _ _ _` and use `erw [hmc]`**
